summary: Gruber revisits his earlier criticism of Andrew Stone, the NeXT veteran and Cocoa evangelist whose FontSight utility only works with Cocoa applications — a crippling limitation given that professional design software is Carbon-based. Gruber initially accused Stone of deliberate dishonesty but now concludes Stone is genuinely delusional, stuck in a 1997 mindset where NeXT's framework was supposed to replace the Mac OS entirely. Stone refuses to support Carbon apps despite customers explicitly telling him that's what they need, while competitor Unsanity is building FontCard to work with both APIs. Gruber argues that end users don't care about developer APIs — they care about features and compatibility with their existing tools. The result is that Stone Design loses a market opportunity and Mac users lose a potentially useful utility.
key_points: Stone's FontSight only works with Cocoa apps, excluding nearly all professional design software, which is its natural market
Stone's worldview is frozen circa 1997, when Apple's original plan was to replace the Mac OS with NeXT's framework and no Carbon layer existed
Competitor Unsanity's FontCard will support both Cocoa and Carbon apps, making FontSight's limitation a fatal competitive disadvantage
End users choose software based on features and compatibility, not which developer API was used to build it
Stone is actively refusing customer requests to support Carbon, which is irrational from both a business and technical perspective
money_quotes: Andrew Stone is out of his fucking mind.
Potential customers are telling him exactly what they want FontSight to do to make it useful for them; and Stone is responding by saying he absolutely will not do that. That simply isn't rational.
People aren't going to abandon their software from Adobe, Microsoft, and Macromedia just to use a Cocoa-only menu font utility.

Stone's argument that Carbon applications are irrelevant and/or deprecated hinges on the word of Gil Amelio? Gil Amelio? What Gil Amelio said in 1997 is about as relevant today as something uttered by Gerald Ford in 1975.
Carbon and Cocoa are developer APIs; they are of no interest whatsoever to normal Mac users. None. Really.

Normal Mac users choose their software based on tangible surface qualities — the features, the human interface, their existing toolset, etc. They don't care what programming tools were used to create their software any more than they care what CAD program was used to design their automobiles.
Stone's entire argument against supporting Carbon applications in FontSight is about developer APIs that users don't care about.
It's not something to get angry about. It's just sad.
key_insight: Letting ideological attachment to a developer framework override what customers actually need is both bad business and a fundamental misunderstanding of what users care about.
